meta.title: Software Engineering
meta.subtitle: build quality software
meta.blurb: From clean code and design patterns to testing, API design, concurrency, and architecture — the craft of building maintainable, reliable software systems.

t-clean-code.label: Clean Code
t-clean-code.desc: Writing readable, maintainable code — naming, functions, formatting, and the art of clarity.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: Code is read 10x more than it's written. **Clean code** is simple, direct, and reads like well-written prose. Good names, small functions, and consistent formatting are the foundation.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: **Names** should reveal intent (no abbreviations, no mental mapping). **Functions** should do one thing, be short, and have few parameters. **Comments** should explain *why*, never *what* (the code shows what). Eliminate duplication ruthlessly.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: Ousterhout's key insight: complexity comes from **dependencies** and **obscurity**. Design modules with deep interfaces (simple API, complex implementation hidden). The goal is reducing the cognitive load on the next reader — often future you.

t-solid.label: SOLID Principles
t-solid.desc: Five principles for flexible, maintainable object-oriented design.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: **S**ingle Responsibility: one reason to change. **O**pen/Closed: open for extension, closed for modification. **L**iskov Substitution: subtypes must be substitutable. **I**nterface Segregation: small, focused interfaces. **D**ependency Inversion: depend on abstractions.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: SOLID reduces **coupling** (dependencies between modules) and increases **cohesion** (related things together). The result: changes propagate locally, not globally. Apply at class, module, and service boundaries.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: SOLID isn't dogma — it's a set of heuristics. Over-applying them creates needless abstraction layers. Use them when you feel pain: a class doing too much (SRP), a change requiring edits in many places (OCP/DIP), or an interface forcing implementors to stub methods (ISP).

t-data-structures.label: Data Structures
t-data-structures.desc: Choosing the right container for the job — arrays, hash maps, trees, graphs, and their trade-offs.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: **Arrays** for ordered, indexed access. **Hash maps** for O(1) key lookup. **Sets** for membership. **Stacks/Queues** for LIFO/FIFO. Choose by access pattern, not habit.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: **Trees** (BST, B-trees, tries) for sorted data and hierarchies. **Graphs** for relationships. **Heaps** for priority. Know the **time complexity** of operations on each — the wrong structure turns O(1) into O(n).

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: In production code, you rarely implement these from scratch — you choose the right standard library type. Know when a `HashMap` beats a `TreeMap`, when a `Deque` is better than an `ArrayList`, and when you need a concurrent variant. Profiling reveals when the choice matters.

t-algorithms.label: Algorithms & Complexity
t-algorithms.desc: Fundamental algorithms, time/space complexity, and knowing when O(n²) matters.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: **Big-O** describes how runtime/space grows with input size. O(1) < O(log n) < O(n) < O(n log n) < O(n²). Know it for your data structures and common operations.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: Core algorithms: **binary search** (sorted data), **BFS/DFS** (graphs), **sorting** (merge, quick), **dynamic programming** (overlapping subproblems). Pattern recognition matters more than memorisation.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: Most production code doesn't need exotic algorithms — but recognising an O(n²) loop that should be O(n) prevents real performance bugs. Profile first, optimise where the data says it matters. Interview prep and production code require different algorithm fluency.

t-design-patterns.label: Design Patterns
t-design-patterns.desc: Reusable solutions to common design problems — creational, structural, and behavioural patterns.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: Design patterns are **named, proven solutions** to recurring problems. They give teams a shared vocabulary: 'use a Strategy here' communicates more than a paragraph of explanation.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: **Creational** (Factory, Builder, Singleton): object creation. **Structural** (Adapter, Decorator, Facade): composing objects. **Behavioural** (Strategy, Observer, Command): interaction between objects. Know the top 10; look up others when the problem fits.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: Don't force patterns — they emerge from the problem. Overuse creates over-engineered, hard-to-follow code (the 'AbstractSingletonProxyFactoryBean' problem). Modern languages often make classic GoF patterns unnecessary (closures replace Strategy, mixins replace Decorator). Use the pattern's *intent*, not necessarily its class structure.

t-architecture-styles.label: Architecture Styles
t-architecture-styles.desc: High-level system structuring — layered, hexagonal, clean architecture, monolith vs microservices.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: Architecture defines the **big boxes and arrows** — how code is organised at the highest level. **Layered** (presentation → business → data) is the simplest. The key principle: **separate what changes for different reasons**.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: **Hexagonal / Ports & Adapters**: business logic in the centre, infrastructure at the edges via interfaces. **Clean Architecture**: concentric circles (entities → use cases → adapters → frameworks), dependencies point inward. Both achieve the same goal: protect domain logic from infrastructure churn.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: Architecture is about **trade-offs**, not right answers. A modular monolith with clean boundaries is often better than premature microservices. Choose based on team size, deployment needs, and change patterns. Over-architecting a simple CRUD app is as harmful as under-architecting a complex domain.

t-ddd.label: Domain-Driven Design
t-ddd.desc: Modelling software around the business domain — ubiquitous language, bounded contexts, and aggregates.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: DDD aligns code structure with the **business domain**. Use the same language as domain experts (**ubiquitous language**) in code, conversations, and docs. Model reflects reality, not database tables.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: **Bounded contexts**: separate models for separate subdomains (an 'Order' means different things to shipping vs billing). **Aggregates**: consistency boundaries within a context. **Domain events**: things that happened that others care about.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: DDD is most valuable in **complex domains** where the business logic is the hard part (not CRUD apps). Bounded contexts map naturally to microservice boundaries. Strategic DDD (context mapping) matters more than tactical DDD (entities, value objects) for most teams. Start with event storming to discover domains.

t-refactoring.label: Refactoring
t-refactoring.desc: Improving code structure without changing behaviour — safely, incrementally, continuously.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: **Refactoring** restructures code without changing its external behaviour. Small, safe steps: rename, extract method, inline, move. Each step keeps tests green. It's how you pay down technical debt continuously.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: Recognise **code smells** (symptoms of deeper problems): long methods, large classes, feature envy, shotgun surgery. Each smell has named refactorings that address it. IDE support (rename, extract, inline) makes mechanical refactoring fast and safe.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: Refactor as you go (‘boy scout rule’: leave code better than you found it), not in big-bang rewrites. Characterisation tests protect legacy code you don't fully understand. The best time to refactor is before adding a feature — reshape the code so the feature drops in easily.

t-rest-api.label: REST API Design
t-rest-api.desc: Designing resource-oriented HTTP APIs — naming, verbs, status codes, pagination, and versioning.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: REST models APIs as **resources** (nouns, not verbs) accessed via HTTP methods: GET (read), POST (create), PUT/PATCH (update), DELETE (remove). Use proper status codes (200, 201, 400, 404, 500).

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: **Naming**: plural nouns (`/users/{id}/orders`), consistent casing. **Pagination**: cursor-based > offset-based at scale. **Filtering/sorting** via query params. **Versioning**: URL path (`/v2/`) or header. Document with **OpenAPI/Swagger**.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: REST is the default for public/external APIs (broadest client compatibility). Design for **evolvability**: additive changes are safe, breaking changes need versioning. Use **HATEOAS** sparingly — it adds complexity most clients don't use. Idempotent operations (PUT, DELETE) simplify retry logic.

t-graphql-api.label: GraphQL
t-graphql-api.desc: Client-driven query APIs — request exactly the data you need, schema-first, strongly typed.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: GraphQL lets clients **ask for exactly what they need** in a single request. A typed **schema** defines available data; clients write queries against it. No over-fetching (too much data) or under-fetching (multiple roundtrips).

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: **Queries** (read), **Mutations** (write), **Subscriptions** (real-time). **Resolvers** fetch data per field. **Schema-first** design: define the schema as the contract, implement resolvers behind it. Fragments for reuse, variables for parameterisation.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: GraphQL shines for complex UIs with varied data needs (dashboards, mobile apps). Watch for: **N+1 queries** (use DataLoader), **query complexity attacks** (limit depth/cost), and **caching difficulty** (no URL-based caching like REST). Federation (Apollo) scales GraphQL across multiple services.

t-grpc-api.label: gRPC & Protobuf
t-grpc-api.desc: High-performance RPC with protocol buffers — type-safe, streaming, and efficient for service-to-service.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: **gRPC** uses HTTP/2 + Protocol Buffers for fast, typed service-to-service communication. Define services in `.proto` files; generate client and server code in any language. Binary encoding = smaller, faster than JSON.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: Four patterns: **unary** (request-response), **server streaming**, **client streaming**, **bidirectional streaming**. Built-in features: deadlines, cancellation, metadata, interceptors. Schema evolution: add fields without breaking existing clients.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: gRPC dominates internal microservice communication (speed, type safety, codegen). Not ideal for browser clients (limited support) — use gRPC-Web or a REST gateway for external APIs. Design proto schemas carefully — they're your API contract and hard to change once deployed widely.

t-event-apis.label: Event-Driven APIs
t-event-apis.desc: Asynchronous communication via events — webhooks, WebSockets, SSE, and async API design.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: Not all APIs are request-response. **Webhooks** (server pushes to your URL on events), **WebSockets** (persistent bidirectional channel), and **SSE** (server pushes stream of events) enable real-time, event-driven integration.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: **Webhooks**: simple but need retry logic, signature verification, and idempotent handlers. **WebSockets**: full-duplex, good for chat/gaming but complex to scale. **SSE**: simpler than WebSockets for server-to-client streaming (auto-reconnect built into browsers). **AsyncAPI** spec documents event-driven interfaces.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: Use webhooks for integrations (simple, firewall-friendly). Use SSE for one-way streaming (live feeds, AI token streaming). Use WebSockets only when bidirectional real-time is genuinely needed. For internal systems, Kafka/event streams replace all of these with durable, replayable messaging.

t-unit-testing.label: Unit Testing
t-unit-testing.desc: Testing individual units of code in isolation — fast, focused, and the foundation of confidence.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: A **unit test** verifies a small piece of logic in isolation. Fast (milliseconds), deterministic, no external dependencies. Structure: **Arrange** (set up), **Act** (execute), **Assert** (verify).

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: **Mocks/stubs** replace external dependencies (databases, APIs) for isolation. Focus on **behaviour**, not implementation details — tests coupled to internals break on every refactor. Aim for meaningful coverage, not 100% line coverage.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: The best unit tests protect against **regressions** without hindering refactoring. Test public behaviour (inputs → outputs), not private methods. The testing trophy (Dodds): mostly integration tests, some unit tests, few E2E. Over-mocking creates brittle tests that pass even when the system is broken.

t-integration-testing.label: Integration & E2E Testing
t-integration-testing.desc: Testing how components work together — databases, APIs, and full user flows.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: **Integration tests** verify that components work together correctly (your code + real database, your service + another service). Slower than unit tests but catch wiring bugs that unit tests miss.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: **Testcontainers**: spin up real databases/services in Docker for tests. **Contract testing** (Pact): verify API compatibility between services without end-to-end environments. **E2E tests** (Playwright, Cypress): simulate real user flows through the full stack.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: Integration tests give the most confidence per test. Use Testcontainers for database tests (fast, real, disposable). Contract tests prevent microservice integration breakages without slow E2E suites. Keep E2E tests few and focused on critical user paths — they're slow and flaky at scale.

t-tdd.label: Test-Driven Development
t-tdd.desc: Writing tests before code — red-green-refactor as a design and development discipline.

links.title: TDD: Where Did It All Go Wrong (Ian Cooper)

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: **TDD cycle**: write a failing test (**red**), write the minimum code to pass it (**green**), then improve the design (**refactor**). Tests drive the design, not just verify it.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: TDD forces you to think about the **interface** before the implementation. Small cycles keep you focused and provide instant feedback. Tests written first tend to be better tests — they test behaviour, not implementation details.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: TDD isn't all-or-nothing. Use it when: exploring complex logic, working on critical paths, or when design isn't clear. Skip it for: trivial code, UI layout, or when spiking/prototyping. The key insight (Ian Cooper): test behaviours through the public API, not internals — or refactoring becomes painful.

t-code-quality.label: Code Quality Tools
t-code-quality.desc: Automated quality enforcement — linters, formatters, static analysis, and quality gates in CI.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: Automate what humans shouldn't debate: **formatters** (Prettier, Black, Ruff) enforce style automatically. **Linters** (ESLint, pylint) catch bugs and anti-patterns. Run both in CI so every PR meets the bar.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: **Static analysis** (SonarQube, Semgrep, CodeQL) finds security vulnerabilities, complexity hotspots, and code smells without running the code. **Type checkers** (TypeScript, mypy, Pyright) catch type errors at build time.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: Quality gates in CI (must pass linting, tests, coverage threshold, no critical static analysis findings) prevent regression. In 2026, **AI coding assistants** (GitHub Copilot, Cursor, Augment) achieve 84% developer adoption — they generate code, suggest fixes, and run as autonomous agents that plan, execute, and open PRs. But AI-generated code still requires review: trust is at 29% despite high usage. Automated quality checks become more important, not less, as AI increases code velocity.

t-concurrency.label: Concurrency & Parallelism
t-concurrency.desc: Doing multiple things at once — threads, locks, races, and models for safe concurrent programming.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: **Concurrency**: dealing with many things at once (structure). **Parallelism**: doing many things at once (execution). **Threads** share memory; that sharing creates races, deadlocks, and subtle bugs.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: **Mutual exclusion** (locks/mutexes) protects shared state but introduces contention and deadlock risk. Alternatives: **immutability** (nothing to protect), **message passing** (actors, channels — Go, Erlang), **STM** (software transactional memory). Know your language's concurrency model.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: The safest concurrent code avoids shared mutable state entirely. Prefer: immutable data, thread-local state, and communication via channels/queues. When you must share state, use the highest-level abstraction available (concurrent collections, atomic operations) before reaching for raw locks. Test with thread sanitisers (TSan).

t-async-programming.label: Async Programming
t-async-programming.desc: Non-blocking I/O — async/await, event loops, promises, and writing efficient I/O-bound code.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: **Async programming** lets a single thread handle many I/O operations without blocking. While waiting for a database/API response, the thread does other work. Key mechanism: **async/await** (JS, Python, C#, Rust).

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: An **event loop** schedules async tasks cooperatively. **Promises/Futures** represent values that will arrive later. Don't mix blocking calls in async code (it blocks the entire loop). Understand the difference between **CPU-bound** (use threads/processes) and **I/O-bound** (use async) work.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: Async is essential for high-concurrency servers (web servers, API gateways, chat systems). But it adds complexity: error handling, debugging, and testing are harder. Use async for I/O-bound services (many network calls); use threading/multiprocessing for CPU-bound work. Structured concurrency (Python 3.11+ TaskGroups, Kotlin coroutines) makes async safer.

t-performance.label: Performance Engineering
t-performance.desc: Making software fast — profiling, benchmarking, caching, and knowing where time actually goes.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: **Don't guess, measure.** Profile before optimising. Most performance problems are in a tiny fraction of code. Use profilers to find the hotspot, then fix it. Premature optimisation is the root of all evil (Knuth).

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: **CPU profiling** (where time is spent), **memory profiling** (allocations, leaks), **I/O profiling** (disk, network waits). **Flame graphs** visualise call stacks and time. **Benchmarks** with statistical rigour (not single-run timings).

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: Common wins: reduce allocations, batch I/O, cache computed results, use appropriate data structures, eliminate N+1 queries. At scale: connection pooling, async I/O, memory-mapped files, SIMD. Always establish a **performance budget** and measure in CI to prevent regression. Latency percentiles (p50, p95, p99) matter more than averages.

t-memory-management.label: Memory Management
t-memory-management.desc: How programs use memory — stack vs heap, garbage collection, ownership, and preventing leaks.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: **Stack**: fast, automatic, fixed-size (function locals). **Heap**: flexible, manual or GC-managed, for dynamic data. Memory leaks happen when heap memory is allocated but never freed (or references are held unnecessarily).

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: **Garbage collection** (Java, Go, JS, Python): runtime automatically frees unreachable objects. Trade-off: convenience vs GC pauses. **Manual management** (C/C++): full control but dangling pointers and use-after-free bugs. **Ownership** (Rust): compile-time memory safety without GC.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: In GC languages: avoid excessive allocations (object pools for hot paths), watch for reference leaks (event listeners, caches without eviction), and tune GC if latency-sensitive. In Rust: the borrow checker is strict but eliminates entire classes of bugs. Understand your language's model — it shapes how you design data flow.

t-version-control.label: Version Control (Git)
t-version-control.desc: Managing code history — branching strategies, commits, collaboration workflows, and git internals.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: **Git** tracks every change to your codebase. **Commits** are snapshots. **Branches** isolate work. **Merging/rebasing** integrates changes. Master the basics: add, commit, branch, merge, push, pull.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: **Branching strategies**: trunk-based development (short-lived branches, frequent merges) vs GitFlow (long-lived branches). **Conventional commits** standardise messages for changelogs. **Interactive rebase** cleans up history before merging.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: **Trunk-based development** is the standard for high-performing teams: short-lived feature branches (<1 day), frequent integration to main, feature flags for incomplete work. Combined with CI/CD, this enables deployment frequency that correlates with DORA elite performance. In 2026, AI agents (GitHub Agent Mode, Cursor background agents) can operate on branches autonomously — making clean git history and clear commit messages even more important for collaboration between humans and AI.

t-code-review.label: Code Review
t-code-review.desc: Reviewing code effectively — what to look for, how to give feedback, and making reviews efficient.

links.title: How to Make Your Code Reviewer Fall in Love with You

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: Code review catches bugs, shares knowledge, and maintains quality. Review for: **correctness**, **clarity**, **maintainability**, and **security**. Be kind, be specific, and suggest alternatives when critiquing.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: **Small PRs** (<400 lines) get better reviews. Focus on design and logic, not style (automate style with formatters). Distinguish between blocking issues (must fix), suggestions (nice to have), and nits (take or leave). As author: provide context, self-review first, keep changes focused.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: Code review catches design issues, knowledge gaps, and subtle bugs that linters miss. In 2026, AI review assistants (CodeRabbit, GitHub Copilot code review) provide first-pass automated feedback on PRs, but human review remains essential for architectural decisions, security implications, and domain-specific correctness. Keep reviews small (<400 lines), timely (<24 hours), and focused on substance over style (let formatters handle style).

t-documentation.label: Documentation
t-documentation.desc: Writing useful docs — READMEs, ADRs, API docs, and documentation as a product.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: Good documentation answers: **what** (README), **why** (ADRs, design docs), and **how** (API docs, tutorials). Write for the audience: new joiner, future maintainer, or external consumer.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: The Divío system: four kinds of docs — **tutorials** (learning-oriented), **how-to guides** (task-oriented), **reference** (information-oriented), **explanation** (understanding-oriented). **ADRs** (Architecture Decision Records) capture the *why* behind design choices.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: Documentation rots fast. Keep it close to code (doc comments, README in the repo), auto-generate what you can (API docs from OpenAPI/proto), and treat docs like code (review, test links, version). The best documentation is the minimum needed to prevent repeated questions and onboarding friction.

t-tech-debt.label: Technical Debt
t-tech-debt.desc: Understanding, tracking, and strategically managing the cost of shortcuts in code.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: **Technical debt** is the accumulated cost of shortcuts: code that works but slows future development. Like financial debt, it compounds — ignore it long enough and all velocity goes to interest payments.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: **Deliberate** debt (conscious shortcut for speed) vs **inadvertent** (didn't know better). Track it explicitly (tickets, code annotations). Prioritise by **impact on velocity** — not all debt is equal. Some is in cold code that never changes; some blocks every feature.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: Don't aim for zero debt — that's over-engineering. Pay down debt **strategically**: before adding features in that area, during planned maintenance windows, or when it's causing incidents. Allocate 15–20% of sprint capacity to debt reduction. Make the cost visible to stakeholders in business terms (slower features, more bugs, longer onboarding).
